meta.notes: AI-generated proof-of-concept CPBS items for SpeedyCAT (chunk 4 of 10). NOT real MCAT/AAMC content and NOT from any answer key. Every stem and choice is plain text (no HTML/SVG/LaTeX). Answer keys were computed programmatically and each item passed a 4-criteria eval (four distinct choices; clean plain-text formatting; MCAT-appropriate difficulty; exactly one correct choice).

questions.stem: What is the equilibrium constant expression K for the reaction PCl5 ⇌ PCl3 + Cl2?
questions.explanation: K places products over reactants, each concentration raised to its coefficient: K = [PCl3][Cl2] / [PCl5].
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: In general, how is the equilibrium constant K written for a reaction?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Reactants divided by products, each raised to its coefficient

choices.label: B
choices.text: Products divided by reactants, each concentration raised to its stoichiometric coefficient

choices.label: C
choices.text: The sum of the product concentrations minus the reactant concentrations

choices.label: D
choices.text: Products divided by reactants, but ignoring all stoichiometric coefficients

hints.rationale: K is products over reactants, each concentration raised to its coefficient.

hints.prompt: In the K expression for PCl5 forming PCl3 and Cl2, which species belong in the numerator (top)?
hints.rationale: Products go on top, so PCl3 and Cl2 are in the numerator.

hints.prompt: Which listed expression is a trap because it is the correct K for a DIFFERENT reaction (the one in which NO2 forms N2O4)?
hints.rationale: [N2O4] / [NO2]^2 is the K for 2 NO2 forming N2O4, not for this reaction, so it is a trap.

questions.stem: An ideal fluid flows at 0.5 m/s through a pipe of cross-section 5 cm^2. What is its speed where the cross-section narrows to 1 cm^2?
questions.explanation: Continuity: A1 v1 = A2 v2, so v2 = (5)(0.5)/1 = 2.5 m/s.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: Which principle governs how an ideal (incompressible) fluid's speed changes as the pipe's cross-sectional area changes?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The continuity equation: A1 v1 = A2 v2

choices.label: B
choices.text: Speed is directly proportional to cross-sectional area

choices.label: D
choices.text: The ideal gas law: P V = n R T

hints.rationale: For an incompressible fluid the volume flow rate A v is conserved.

hints.prompt: Using A1 v1 = A2 v2 with v1 = 0.5 m/s, A1 = 5 cm^2, and A2 = 1 cm^2, which setup gives v2?
hints.rationale: Solve A1 v1 = A2 v2 for v2 = A1 v1 / A2.

hints.prompt: Because the pipe narrows, the speed must increase; which two choices can be ruled out on that basis?
hints.rationale: A smaller area forces a higher speed, so a value equal to or below the original 0.5 m/s is impossible.

questions.stem: What is the maximum number of electrons in a d subshell?
questions.explanation: A d subshell holds at most 10 electrons.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: What determines the maximum number of electrons a subshell can hold?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Two electrons per orbital, times the number of orbitals in the subshell

choices.label: D
choices.text: It equals the principal quantum number n

hints.rationale: Each orbital holds 2 electrons, so capacity = 2 x (number of orbitals).

hints.prompt: How many orbitals does a d subshell contain?
hints.rationale: A d subshell has 5 orbitals (l = 2 gives 2l+1 = 5).

hints.prompt: Which two choices can be eliminated because they are the capacities of the s and f subshells, not the d subshell?
hints.rationale: s holds 2 and f holds 14; those belong to different subshells, so they can be ruled out.

questions.stem: An object starts at 5 m/s and accelerates uniformly at 2 m/s^2 for 5 s. What is its final velocity?
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: Which kinematic equation gives final velocity from initial velocity, constant acceleration, and time?
hints.rationale: For constant acceleration, v = u + a t.

hints.prompt: With u = 5 m/s, a = 2 m/s^2, and t = 5 s, which setup gives v?
hints.rationale: Add the velocity gained (a t) to the initial velocity u.

hints.prompt: Which value is the trap from multiplying all three numbers (5 x 2 x 5), treating v as u a t?
hints.rationale: v = u a t is not a valid relation; it gives 50 m/s, a trap for the correct v = u + a t.

questions.stem: A 10 μF capacitor is charged to 100 V. How much charge does it store?
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: Which relationship connects charge Q, capacitance C, and voltage V?
hints.rationale: Charge stored on a capacitor is Q = C V.

hints.prompt: With C = 10 microfarads and V = 100 V, which setup gives Q (in microcoulombs)?
hints.rationale: Multiply capacitance by voltage: Q = C V.

hints.prompt: Which value is the trap from adding C and V (10 + 100) instead of multiplying them?
hints.rationale: Adding instead of multiplying gives 110 uC, a trap; Q = C V requires multiplication.

questions.stem: An object is 60 cm from a thin converging lens of focal length 20 cm. Where is the image formed?
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: Which equation relates focal length f, object distance do, and image distance di for a thin lens?
hints.rationale: The thin-lens equation is 1/f = 1/do + 1/di.

hints.prompt: With f = 20 cm and do = 60 cm, which setup gives 1/di?
hints.rationale: Rearrange the lens equation: 1/di = 1/f - 1/do.

hints.prompt: Which value is a trap equal to the focal length itself, from forgetting to subtract 1/do?
hints.rationale: 20 cm just repeats f; the image distance requires solving 1/di = 1/f - 1/do.

questions.stem: What is the hybridization of the central atom in C2H2 (ethyne)?
questions.explanation: The electron-domain geometry of C2H2 (ethyne) corresponds to sp hybridization of the central atom.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: How is the hybridization of a central atom determined?

choices.label: A
choices.text: By the number of electron domains (bonded atoms plus lone pairs) around it

choices.label: B
choices.text: By the total number of atoms in the molecule

choices.label: D
choices.text: By the number of protons in the central atom

hints.rationale: Hybridization follows the count of electron domains around the central atom.

hints.prompt: In ethyne (H-C triple bond C-H), each carbon bonds to one H and one C (the triple bond counts as one domain). How many sigma domains is that?
hints.rationale: Each carbon has 2 sigma domains (one to H and one to the other carbon).

hints.prompt: A carbon with only 2 sigma domains cannot use a hybridization implying 4 or 5 domains; which two choices can be eliminated?
hints.rationale: sp3 implies 4 domains and sp3d implies 5; a 2-domain carbon can be neither.

questions.stem: How many moles are in 64 g of O2? (molar mass 32 g/mol)
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: How do you convert a mass in grams to a number of moles?
hints.rationale: Divide the mass by the molar mass to get moles.

hints.prompt: With mass = 64 g and molar mass = 32 g/mol, which setup gives the number of moles?
hints.rationale: moles = mass / molar mass = 64 / 32.

hints.prompt: Which two values can be eliminated because multiplying or adding mass and molar mass gives absurdly large results?
hints.rationale: 64 x 32 and 64 + 32 are far too large; the number of moles here must be small.

questions.stem: If 0.05 L of 3 M solution is diluted to 0.5 L, what is the new concentration?
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: Which equation describes dilution (adding solvent) at a constant number of moles of solute?
hints.rationale: Moles are conserved on dilution: M1 V1 = M2 V2.

hints.prompt: With M1 = 3 M, V1 = 0.05 L, and V2 = 0.5 L, which setup gives M2?
Because the solution is diluted (volume increases), the concentration must drop; which two choices can be eliminated?
hints.rationale: Dilution lowers concentration below 3 M, so the unchanged and larger values are impossible.

questions.stem: According to Le Chatelier's principle, removing a reactant from a system at equilibrium generally

choices.label: A
choices.text: shifts toward the side with fewer moles of gas.

choices.label: B
choices.text: shifts the equilibrium toward the products.

choices.label: C
choices.text: shifts the equilibrium toward the reactants.

choices.label: D
choices.text: shifts toward the side with more moles of gas.

questions.explanation: The system opposes the change: removing a reactant from a system at equilibrium shifts the equilibrium toward the reactants.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: What does Le Chatelier's principle predict when a stress is applied to a system at equilibrium?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The system shifts to partially oppose (counteract) the change

choices.label: B
choices.text: The system shifts to amplify the change

hints.rationale: The system responds so as to counteract the imposed change.

hints.prompt: In general, when you remove a substance from an equilibrium, the system shifts toward which side?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The side of the substance you removed (to replace it)

choices.label: B
choices.text: The opposite side from what you removed

hints.rationale: The system shifts toward the side that was depleted, to partially restore it.

hints.prompt: Which two choices can be eliminated because this stress is about removing a reactant, not about changing the number of moles of gas (pressure)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'shifts toward the side with fewer moles of gas' and 'shifts toward the side with more moles of gas'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'shifts the equilibrium toward the products' and 'shifts the equilibrium toward the reactants'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'shifts toward the side with fewer moles of gas' and 'shifts the equilibrium toward the reactants'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'shifts toward the side with more moles of gas' and 'shifts the equilibrium toward the products'

hints.rationale: Mole-of-gas arguments apply to pressure or volume changes, not to removing a reactant.

questions.stem: An ideal fluid flows at 0.5 m/s through a pipe of cross-sectional area 2 m^2. What is the volume flow rate?
questions.explanation: Flow rate Q = A v = (2)(0.5) = 1 m^3/s.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: How is volume flow rate related to cross-sectional area A and speed v?
hints.rationale: Volume flow rate is Q = A v.

hints.prompt: With A = 2 m^2 and v = 0.5 m/s, which setup gives the flow rate Q?
hints.rationale: Multiply area by speed: Q = A v.

hints.prompt: Which value is the trap from dividing area by speed (2 / 0.5) instead of multiplying?
hints.rationale: 2 / 0.5 = 4 is a trap; flow rate uses Q = A v (multiplication).

questions.stem: What is the atomic number of carbon?
questions.explanation: The atomic number equals the number of protons; carbon has 6.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: What does an element's atomic number represent?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The number of protons in the nucleus

choices.label: C
choices.text: The total number of protons and neutrons

hints.rationale: The atomic number equals the number of protons.

hints.prompt: Where do you find carbon's atomic number, and what does it equal?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It is the whole number listed for C on the periodic table, equal to its proton count

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is carbon's average atomic mass (about 12)

choices.label: C
choices.text: It is the number of bonds carbon usually forms

hints.rationale: The atomic number is the integer shown for C on the periodic table (its proton count).

hints.prompt: Which two choices can be eliminated because they are far too large or small to be carbon's proton count (a light nonmetal early in the table)?
hints.rationale: 20 is calcium's number and 2 is helium's; carbon sits between them, so those are impossible.

questions.stem: An object with initial velocity 2 m/s accelerates at 3 m/s^2 for 6 s. How far does it travel?
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: Which equation gives displacement under constant acceleration?
hints.rationale: Displacement is d = u t + (1/2) a t^2.

hints.prompt: With u = 2, a = 3, and t = 6, which setup gives d?
hints.rationale: Add the u t term to the (1/2) a t^2 term.

hints.prompt: Which value is the trap from using only (1/2) a t^2 and forgetting the u t term?
hints.rationale: Dropping the u t term (12 m) gives 54 m, a trap; the full displacement includes both terms.

questions.stem: Two parallel plates 0.1 m apart have a potential difference of 12 V. What is the electric field between them?
questions.explanation: For parallel plates, E = V / d = 12 / 0.1 = 120 V/m.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: For uniform parallel plates, how is the field E related to voltage V and plate separation d?
hints.rationale: Between parallel plates, E = V / d.

hints.prompt: With V = 12 V and d = 0.1 m, which setup gives E?
hints.rationale: Divide voltage by separation: E = V / d.

hints.prompt: Which value is the trap from multiplying V and d (12 x 0.1) instead of dividing?
hints.rationale: 12 x 0.1 = 1.2 is a trap; E = V / d requires division.

questions.stem: For a lens, the object distance is 10 cm and the image distance is 60 cm. What is the magnification?
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: How is lateral magnification related to image distance di and object distance do?
hints.rationale: Magnification is m = -di / do.

hints.prompt: With di = 60 cm and do = 10 cm, which setup gives m?
For a real image from a converging lens the magnification is negative (inverted); which two positive choices can be eliminated?
hints.rationale: A real, inverted image has a negative magnification, so the positive values are impossible.

questions.stem: How many valence electrons does a neutral nitrogen (N) atom have?
questions.explanation: nitrogen (N) is in a main group whose number of valence electrons is 5.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: For a main-group element, how do you find the number of valence electrons?
hints.rationale: For main-group elements the valence electron count comes from the group number.

hints.prompt: Nitrogen sits in the group headed by N and P; which statement helps fix its valence count?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It is in group 15 (5A), the nitrogen family

hints.rationale: Nitrogen is in group 15 (5A).

hints.prompt: Which two choices can be eliminated because a neutral group-15 atom is neither nearly empty (1) nor a full octet (8)?
hints.rationale: A group-15 atom is partway across the row, so 1 (like group 1) and 8 (a full shell) are impossible.

questions.stem: What is the mass of 4 mol of HCl? (molar mass 36.5 g/mol)
questions.explanation: mass = moles x molar mass = 4 x 36.5 = 146 g.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: How do you convert a number of moles to a mass in grams?
hints.rationale: Multiply moles by molar mass to get grams.

hints.prompt: With 4 mol and molar mass 36.5 g/mol, which setup gives the mass?
hints.rationale: mass = moles x molar mass = 4 x 36.5.

hints.prompt: Which value is the trap from adding moles and molar mass (4 + 36.5)?
hints.rationale: 4 + 36.5 = 40.5 g is a trap; mass = moles x molar mass.

questions.stem: A solution has pH = 4. What is its hydrogen ion concentration [H+]?
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: How is the hydrogen-ion concentration obtained from pH?
For pH = 4, which setup gives [H+]?
A pH of 4 is moderately acidic, so [H+] is neither tiny (10^-10) nor impossibly huge (10^4 M); which two choices can be eliminated?
hints.rationale: 10^-10 M is basic-range and 10^4 M is physically impossible, so both are out.

questions.stem: Which factor does NOT increase the rate of a typical reaction?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increasing the surface area of a solid reactant.

questions.explanation: Rate rises with concentration, temperature, catalysts, and surface area; lowering temperature slows it.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: In general, what happens to reaction rate when the temperature is raised?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It increases (more frequent, more energetic collisions)

hints.rationale: Higher temperature raises reaction rate.

hints.prompt: Increasing concentration, adding a catalyst, and increasing surface area all do what to the reaction rate?
hints.rationale: Concentration, catalysts, and surface area all increase reaction rate, so none of them is the exception asked for.

hints.prompt: The exception must be the factor that slows a reaction; which two clearly rate-increasing choices can be eliminated first?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Increasing reactant concentration and adding a catalyst

choices.label: B
choices.text: Lowering the temperature and adding a catalyst

choices.label: C
choices.text: Increasing the surface area of a solid reactant and lowering the temperature

choices.label: D
choices.text: Increasing reactant concentration and lowering the temperature

hints.rationale: Concentration and catalysts speed reactions up, so neither can be the factor that fails to increase rate.

questions.stem: Heat spontaneously flows

choices.label: A
choices.text: from low to high pressure regions.

choices.label: B
choices.text: only when the objects are the same temperature.

choices.label: C
choices.text: from a colder object to a hotter object.

choices.label: D
choices.text: from a hotter object to a colder object.

questions.explanation: Without external work, heat flows from high to low temperature until equilibrium.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: What drives spontaneous heat flow between two objects?
hints.rationale: Heat flows spontaneously because of a temperature difference.

hints.prompt: According to the second law of thermodynamics, spontaneous heat flow does what to the two objects' temperatures over time?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Brings them toward the same temperature (thermal equilibrium)

hints.rationale: Spontaneous heat flow moves systems toward thermal equilibrium.

hints.prompt: Which two choices can be eliminated because heat depends on temperature (not pressure) and never spontaneously widens a temperature gap?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'from low to high pressure regions' and 'from a colder object to a hotter object'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'from a hotter object to a colder object' and 'only when the objects are the same temperature'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'from low to high pressure regions' and 'from a hotter object to a colder object'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'from a colder object to a hotter object' and 'only when the objects are the same temperature'

hints.rationale: Heat is not driven by pressure, and it never spontaneously flows from cold to hot, so those two are out.

questions.stem: What is the ground-state electron configuration of a neutral carbon (Z=6) atom?
questions.explanation: Filling orbitals by the Aufbau principle gives carbon: 1s2 2s2 2p2.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: Which principle dictates the order electrons fill orbitals in a ground-state atom?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The Aufbau principle (lowest-energy orbitals fill first)

choices.label: B
choices.text: Electrons fill the highest-energy orbitals first

choices.label: D
choices.text: All orbitals fill equally at the same time

hints.rationale: Ground-state filling follows the Aufbau order (lowest energy first).

hints.prompt: A neutral carbon atom (Z = 6) must place how many total electrons, filling 2s before 2p?
hints.rationale: Carbon has 6 electrons filling 1s, then 2s, then 2p in order.

hints.prompt: Which two configurations can be eliminated because their superscripts do not add up to 6 electrons?
hints.rationale: Carbon needs exactly 6 electrons; configurations summing to 5 or 8 are impossible.

questions.stem: Starting at 10 m/s, an object accelerates at 5 m/s^2 over a distance of 5 m. What is its final speed?
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: Which kinematic equation links final speed to initial speed, acceleration, and distance (no time)?
hints.rationale: The time-free equation is v^2 = u^2 + 2 a d.

hints.prompt: With u = 10, a = 5, and d = 5, which setup gives v^2 (before taking the square root)?
The speed rises only modestly above the initial 10 m/s, so which two values are far too large to be correct?
hints.rationale: The final speed is just above 10 m/s, so 35 and 50 m/s are far too large.

questions.stem: How much energy is stored in a 2 μF capacitor charged to 5 V?
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: How is the energy stored in a capacitor related to C and V?
hints.rationale: Stored energy is U = (1/2) C V^2.

hints.prompt: With C = 2 microfarads and V = 5 V, which setup gives U?
Which value is the trap from forgetting to square the voltage (using (1/2) C V instead of (1/2) C V^2)?
hints.rationale: (1/2)(2)(5) = 5 uJ omits squaring V; the correct formula squares the voltage.

questions.stem: After 3 half-lives, what fraction of a radioactive sample remains?
questions.explanation: Each half-life halves the amount: fraction = (1/2)^3 = 0.125.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: What happens to the remaining amount of a radioisotope after each half-life?
hints.rationale: Each half-life multiplies the remaining amount by 1/2.

hints.prompt: After 3 half-lives, which expression gives the fraction remaining?
hints.rationale: Fraction remaining = (1/2) raised to the number of half-lives = (1/2)^3.

hints.prompt: Which value is the trap for the fraction that has DECAYED (1 minus the remaining), rather than the fraction remaining?
hints.rationale: 0.875 is the fraction that decayed; the question asks for the fraction remaining.

questions.stem: The bond in KF has an electronegativity difference of about 3.2. It is best classified as
questions.explanation: An electronegativity difference near 3.2 indicates a ionic bond (roughly: 0 nonpolar covalent, up to about 1.7 polar covalent, larger values ionic).
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: Which property of a bond is used to classify it as ionic, polar covalent, or nonpolar covalent?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The electronegativity difference between the atoms

hints.rationale: Bond character is judged by the electronegativity difference.

hints.prompt: Using the usual cutoffs (about 0 to 0.4 nonpolar, 0.4 to 1.7 polar covalent, greater than 1.7 ionic), a difference of 3.2 falls in which range?
hints.rationale: 3.2 is far above the ~1.7 cutoff.

hints.prompt: Which two choices can be eliminated because a very large electronegativity difference is neither a metallic bond nor a nonpolar covalent bond?
hints.rationale: Metallic bonding is between metals and nonpolar covalent needs a near-zero difference, so both are ruled out.

questions.stem: How many molecules are in 1 mol of a substance? (Avogadro's number = 6.022 x 10^23 /mol)
questions.explanation: molecules = moles x N_A = 1 x 6.022 x 10^23 = 6.02 x 10^23.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: How do you convert a number of moles to a number of molecules?
hints.rationale: Multiply moles by Avogadro's number (6.022 x 10^23).

hints.prompt: For 1 mole, which setup gives the number of molecules?
hints.rationale: One mole contains exactly Avogadro's number of molecules.

hints.prompt: Which two choices can be eliminated because they are not on the order of Avogadro's number (about 10^23) for one mole?
hints.rationale: One mole gives about 10^23 molecules, so a value near 10^-24 or 10^24 is impossible.

questions.stem: What is the pH of a solution with [H+] = 1 x 10^-4 M?
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: How is pH defined in terms of [H+]?
hints.rationale: pH = -log[H+].

hints.prompt: For [H+] = 1 x 10^-4 M, which setup gives the pH?
Which value is the trap equal to the pOH (14 minus the pH) rather than the pH itself?
hints.rationale: 10 is the pOH for this solution; the question asks for the pH.

questions.stem: How much heat is needed to raise 300 g of water by 2 degrees C? (c = 4.18 J/(g·°C))
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: Which equation gives the heat needed to change a substance's temperature?
hints.rationale: The heat is q = m c (delta T).

hints.prompt: With m = 300 g, c = 4.18, and delta T = 2, which setup gives q?
hints.rationale: Multiply all three: q = m c (delta T).

hints.prompt: Which value is the trap from leaving out the temperature change (using only m c, or delta T = 1)?
hints.rationale: 300 x 4.18 = 1254 J omits the delta T = 2 factor, a trap.

questions.stem: A system absorbs 500 J of heat and does 300 J of work on its surroundings. What is the change in its internal energy?
questions.explanation: First law: ΔU = Q - W = 500 - 300 = 200 J.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: What does the first law of thermodynamics state for the change in internal energy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: (delta U) = Q - W (heat added minus work done by the system)

hints.rationale: First law: (delta U) = Q - W, where W is work done BY the system.

hints.prompt: With Q = 500 J absorbed and W = 300 J done BY the system, which setup gives delta U?
hints.rationale: Subtract the work done by the system from the heat absorbed.

hints.prompt: Which value is the trap from adding Q and W instead of subtracting?
hints.rationale: 500 + 300 = 800 J is a trap; the system did work, so that energy leaves the system.

questions.stem: Which atom has the larger atomic radius, F or I?

choices.label: D
choices.text: atomic radius shows no periodic trend

questions.explanation: I has the larger atomic radius: same group; radius increases down a group, so I is larger.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: How does atomic radius change as you move DOWN a group?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It increases (more electron shells are added)

hints.rationale: Radius increases down a group as electron shells are added.

hints.prompt: F and I are in the same group (the halogens); which is lower in the group with more shells?

choices.label: C
choices.text: They are in the same period

hints.rationale: Iodine is far below fluorine in the halogen group.

hints.prompt: Which two choices can be eliminated because atomic radius does follow a clear periodic trend and the two atoms are not the same size?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'They are identical' and 'atomic radius shows no periodic trend'

hints.prompt: What distinguishes a polar covalent bond from an ionic bond?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Polar covalent bonds share electrons unequally; ionic bonds transfer electrons

choices.label: C
choices.text: Polar covalent bonds occur only between two metals

hints.rationale: Polar covalent means unequal sharing; ionic means electron transfer.

hints.prompt: HF is a bond between two nonmetals (hydrogen and fluorine) with different electronegativities. Nonmetal-nonmetal bonding with unequal sharing is best described as which type?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A covalent bond that is polar

hints.rationale: Two nonmetals share electrons, and the difference makes that sharing unequal (polar).

hints.prompt: Which two choices can be eliminated because HF has a clear electronegativity difference and contains no metal?
hints.rationale: A nonzero difference rules out nonpolar covalent, and having no metal rules out metallic.

questions.stem: How many moles are in 90 g of C6H12O6? (molar mass 180 g/mol)
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: How do you convert a mass in grams to a number of moles?
hints.rationale: Divide the mass by the molar mass.

hints.prompt: With mass = 90 g and molar mass = 180 g/mol, which setup gives the number of moles?
hints.rationale: moles = mass / molar mass = 90 / 180.

hints.prompt: Which two values can be eliminated because multiplying or adding mass and molar mass gives absurdly large results?
hints.rationale: 90 x 180 and 90 + 180 are far too large; the number of moles must be small.

questions.stem: What is the molarity of a solution with 0.1 mol of solute in 0.5 L of solution?
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: How is molarity defined?

choices.label: A
choices.text: moles of solute divided by liters of solution

choices.label: B
choices.text: liters of solution divided by moles of solute

choices.label: C
choices.text: moles of solute times liters of solution

choices.label: D
choices.text: moles of solute plus liters of solution

hints.rationale: Molarity = moles / liters.

hints.prompt: With 0.1 mol of solute in 0.5 L, which setup gives the molarity?
hints.rationale: Divide moles by liters: M = moles / liters.

hints.prompt: Which value is the trap from dividing liters by moles (0.5 / 0.1) instead of moles by liters?
hints.rationale: 0.5 / 0.1 = 5 inverts the ratio; molarity = moles / liters.

questions.stem: According to collision theory, a reaction occurs when particles collide

choices.label: B
choices.text: with sufficient energy and proper orientation.

choices.label: C
choices.text: only if a catalyst is absent.

choices.label: D
choices.text: at any energy regardless of orientation.

questions.explanation: Effective collisions need enough energy and correct orientation to react.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: According to collision theory, what must reactant particles do in order to react?
hints.rationale: Reactions require effective collisions between particles.

hints.prompt: What makes a collision 'effective' (able to produce a reaction)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Enough energy (activation energy) AND a proper orientation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Only the absence of a catalyst

hints.rationale: An effective collision needs sufficient energy and the correct orientation.

hints.prompt: Which two choices can be eliminated because a reaction requires particles to exchange energy and because orientation matters?

choices.label: A
choices.text: 'without exchanging any energy.' and 'at any energy regardless of orientation.'

choices.label: B
choices.text: 'with sufficient energy and proper orientation.' and 'only if a catalyst is absent.'

choices.label: C
choices.text: 'without exchanging any energy.' and 'with sufficient energy and proper orientation.'

choices.label: D
choices.text: 'at any energy regardless of orientation.' and 'only if a catalyst is absent.'

hints.rationale: Collisions with no energy or the wrong orientation do not react, so those conditions are impossible for a reaction.

questions.stem: What is the buoyant force on an object of volume 0.02 m^3 fully submerged in a fluid of density 800 kg/m^3? (g = 9.8 m/s^2)
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

hints.prompt: How is the buoyant force on a fully submerged object calculated (Archimedes' principle)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: F = (rho) V g (the weight of the displaced fluid)

hints.rationale: Buoyant force equals the weight of displaced fluid, F = (rho) V g.

hints.prompt: With rho = 800, V = 0.02, and g = 9.8, which setup gives the buoyant force?
hints.rationale: Multiply the fluid density, the volume, and g.

hints.prompt: Which value is the trap from leaving out g (using density times volume only, 800 x 0.02)?
hints.rationale: 800 x 0.02 = 16 omits the factor g = 9.8; F = (rho) V g.
